subject: Discrete Random Variables
topic: §15 Expected Value of a Discrete Random Variable
question: You have a fancy car video system in your car and you feel you want to insure it against theft. An insurance company offers you a $2000 1-year coverage for a premium of $225. The probability that the theft will occur is 0.1. What is your expected return from this policy?
explanation: Let $X$ be the random variable of the profit/loss from this policy to policyholder. Then either $X = 1{,}775$ with probability $0.1$ or $X = -225$ with porbability $0.9$. Thus, the expected return of this policy is $E(X) = 1{,}775(0.1) + (-225)(0.9) = -\$25$. That is, by insuring the car video system for many years, and under the same circumstances, you will expect a net loss of $25 per year to the insurance company Remark 15.1 The expected value (or mean) is related to the physical property of center of mass. If we have a weightless rod in which weights of mass $\Pr(x)$ located at a distance $x$ from the left endpoint of the rod then the point at which the rod is balanced is called the center of mass. If $\alpha$ is the center of mass then we must have $\sum_x (x - \alpha) p(x) = 0$. This equation implies that $\alpha = \sum_x x p(x) = E(X)$. Thus, the expected value tells us something about the center of the probability mass function.
